solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus with the chain rule, \( F'(x) = \left( ([[a]])\,(x^{[[m]]}) + ([[b]]) \right)\cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{[[m]]}\right) = \left( ([[a]])x^{[[m]]} + ([[b]]) \right)\cdot [[m]]x^{[[m]]-1} \). At \( x = [[x0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( g(u([[x0]])) \) but forgets to multiply by the derivative of the inner function \( u(x) = x^{[[m]]} \).
This evaluates the integrand at \( [[x0]] \) instead of at \( u([[x0]]) = [[x0]]^{[[m]]} \).
This uses \( [[m]]x^{[[m]]} \) for the inner derivative instead of \( [[m]]x^{[[m]]-1} \).
This multiplies by \( [[x0]] \) rather than the full inner derivative \( [[m]]\cdot[[x0]]^{[[m]]-1} \).
This is off from the correct value by the inner exponent.
This is off from the correct value by the inner exponent.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Split \( F(x) = \int_{0}^{x^{3}}(\cdot)\,dt - \int_{0}^{x^{2}}(\cdot)\,dt \). By FTC-1 and the chain rule, \( F'(x) = \left(([[a]])x^{3}+([[b]])\right)\cdot 3x^{2} - \left(([[a]])x^{2}+([[b]])\right)\cdot 2x \). At \( x=[[x0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the upper-bound term and forgets the lower-bound (now variable) contribution entirely.
This keeps only the lower-bound term and forgets the upper-bound contribution entirely.
This adds the two boundary contributions instead of subtracting the lower-bound term.
This forgets to multiply each boundary term by the derivative of that bound.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus with the chain rule, \( F'(x) = \left(([[a]])\sin^{2}(x) + [[b]]\right)\cdot \frac{d}{dx}\sin(x) = \left(([[a]])\sin^{2}(x) + [[b]]\right)\cos(x) \). At \( x=\frac{[[n]]\pi}{2} \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by the inner derivative \( \cos(x) \) altogether.
This evaluates the integrand at \( x \) itself instead of at the upper bound \( \sin(x) \).
This multiplies by \( \sin(x) \), the inner function itself, instead of its derivative \( \cos(x) \).
This mixes up \( \sin \) and \( \cos \) inside the integrand.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by the constant term of the integrand.

stem: Let \( F(x) = \displaystyle\int_{[[c]]}^{1/x} \left( ([[a]])t + ([[b]]) \right) dt \), for \( x \ne 0 \). Find \( F'([[x0]]) \).
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus with the chain rule, \( F'(x) = \left(([[a]])\cdot\frac{1}{x} + ([[b]])\right)\cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = \left(\frac{[[a]]}{x}+([[b]])\right)\cdot\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) \). At \( x=[[x0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by the inner derivative \( -1/x^{2} \) altogether.
This drops the negative sign from the derivative of \( 1/x \).
This evaluates the integrand at \( x \) instead of at the upper bound \( 1/x \).
This divides by \( x \) instead of multiplying by \( -1/x^{2} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the wrong overall sign.
